content.text: Every language model produces a raw score for every token in its vocabulary at each generation step.

is a mechanism that lets callers add a numerical offset to selected tokens' raw scores before the softmax step, directly steering which tokens the model will or will not produce — without any fine-tuning, prompt manipulation, or post-generation filtering.

At the heart of every autoregressive generation step is a vector of unnormalized scores called logits — one score per vocabulary token. The model computes these scores by projecting its final hidden state through an unembedding matrix. Softmax then converts the logits into a probability distribution, and a token is sampled or chosen via argmax from that distribution.

Logit bias inserts an additive correction step between the raw logits and the softmax. Before the softmax is applied, the inference engine looks up each token's ID in a caller-supplied dictionary and adds the corresponding bias value
All tokens not mentioned in the dictionary retain their original logit. The modified logit vector
is then passed to softmax normally:

In the OpenAI Chat Completions API, logit bias is exposed as the logit_bias parameter — a dictionary mapping token IDs (integers) to bias values (floats, typically in the range -100 to 100). A bias of -100 drives the token's effective probability to approximately zero, functionally banning it. A bias of +100 makes the token so dominant that it will nearly always be chosen regardless of context.

control mechanism: it operates on individual token IDs, not on words or semantic categories. Because different models use different vocabularies and tokenizers, the same bias dictionary cannot be transferred between model families without updating the token IDs.

Most generation control strategies work at the prompt level — you describe what you want in the system or user message and hope the model complies. That approach works well for semantic guidance but fails for hard constraints. A model instructed to 'never output the word "error"' may still produce it under adversarial prompts, hallucination pressure, or unusual contexts.

Logit bias enforces constraints at the numerical level, below the semantic layer. No matter what the context says, a token with a bias of -100 cannot be generated. This makes logit bias the correct tool when you need a provable guarantee rather than a probabilistic tendency.
The most common production use cases are vocabulary restriction (limiting output to a fixed set of tokens, such as

for a binary classifier), content safety (banning tokens that would produce harmful or legally problematic text), and constrained structured output (steering the model toward valid JSON field names or enumerated values).

In agentic systems, logit bias is particularly valuable because agents often need to produce machine-parseable output reliably. A function-calling agent that must respond with a specific keyword to trigger a downstream step can use logit bias to ensure that keyword always has a near-certain probability, eliminating parsing failures.
A bias of -100 effectively removes a token from the generatable set. This is the only mechanism in standard LLM inference APIs that can guarantee token-level exclusion without prompt engineering.
Logit bias is an inference-time parameter. Applying it costs nothing in terms of training compute, storage, or deployment complexity.

The bias addition is a sparse vector operation on the logit vector — O(K) where K is the number of biased tokens, which is typically far less than the vocabulary size. It does not add measurable inference time.
Unlike prompt-based constraints, logit bias does not consume context window tokens. It is a side-channel parameter.
Logit bias works alongside temperature, top-p, and top-k. It reshapes the distribution before those filters are applied.

Values between -20 and +20 nudge probabilities without fully banning or forcing tokens, allowing a model to prefer a dialect, style, or domain vocabulary while still being able to deviate when context strongly demands it.
Restricting the output vocabulary to valid JSON tokens at each step is a lightweight alternative to full grammar-guided generation, sufficient for simple structured outputs.

A bias dictionary built for GPT-4o does not transfer to GPT-3.5-turbo or any other model family. Even within the same tokenizer version, multi-character words often map to multiple token IDs — each must be included. Forgetting a tokenization variant (e.g.
) is a common source of bugs.
Banning too many tokens forces the model into unnatural continuations. The model may begin producing awkward workarounds or semantically incoherent text if critical tokens are excluded.
Logit bias operates on token IDs, not meaning. Banning the token for
does not prevent the model from expressing violence using synonyms or multi-token phrases. It is ineffective as a sole content moderation strategy.
The OpenAI API accepts at most 300 token ID entries in logit_bias per request. Vocabulary restriction to a large custom set may require additional output validation.
The exact parameter name and behavior varies: OpenAI calls it
, while Hugging Face transformers exposes it differently. The Anthropic Claude API does not expose logit bias at all.
The OpenAI API clamps values to the range [-100, 100]. Values outside this range are rejected with a validation error.
The transformer forward pass produces a hidden state vector h at the last layer. The unembedding projection multiplies h by the weight matrix W_U to produce the raw logit vector:
The logit bias step then adds the caller-supplied offsets to the appropriate indices. If the logit_bias dictionary contains K entries

attrs.latex: z'_{k_i} = z_{k_i} + b_{k_i} \quad \text{for each } i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}

content.text: . The modified vector is then passed through log-softmax and, depending on the sampling mode, either the argmax is selected (greedy decoding) or a sample is drawn from the resulting distribution.
To understand the quantitative effect: the softmax distribution ratio between two tokens k and j is:

attrs.latex: \frac{P(t_k \mid x)}{P(t_j \mid x)} = e^{z'_k - z'_j} = e^{(z_k - z_j) + (b_k - b_j)}

content.text: to token k while leaving token j unchanged multiplies the relative odds of k over j by
. This is why even moderate positive biases can dominate the distribution in practice.
gives a relative odds factor of
, which is indistinguishable from zero in floating-point arithmetic. This is why -100 is treated as a hard ban: no competing positive logit in any realistic model will overcome it.

The logit bias is applied identically at every generation step. It does not adapt to context — the same biases apply whether the model is generating token 1 or token 500. This is both a strength (predictability) and a limitation (inflexibility when the constraint should apply only in certain contexts).
The diagram below shows where logit bias is injected in the full token generation pipeline and illustrates its effect on the token distribution.

attrs.alt: Logit Bias injection point in token generation pipeline

content.text: The key observation is that logit bias sits between the raw logit vector produced by the transformer and the softmax step. It is a pure post-processing step: the transformer itself runs identically with or without logit bias. This means the technique adds no load to the GPU-bound forward pass — it is trivially cheap arithmetic on the CPU or output-processing path.
Because the bias is added before softmax, temperature scaling, top-p filtering, and top-k filtering all see the
distribution. A token with a bias of -100 will never appear in the top-k or top-p candidates regardless of temperature, because its effective probability is effectively zero.

When a generation step must produce exactly one of a fixed set of tokens — for example, a yes/no classification or a menu selection from [A, B, C, D] — assign a high positive bias to the valid tokens and a large negative bias to everything else. This is more reliable than prompt instructions alone.

In deployments where certain tokens must never appear regardless of user input (profanity lists, competitor brand names under contractual constraints, personally identifiable patterns), a bias of -100 provides a hard guarantee that prompt injection cannot bypass.
When generating JSON, SQL, or other structured formats, biasing the model toward valid syntactic tokens (e.g., encouraging
tokens in JSON mode) reduces parse errors without requiring a full grammar engine.
A documentation generator can bias toward formal vocabulary tokens and away from casual contractions, producing consistently formal prose without fine-tuning.
A medical summarization tool can soft-bias toward accepted clinical terminology tokens and away from colloquial synonyms, improving terminology consistency in generated reports.

. Banning the token for the word "free" to prevent free-tier references will also suppress legitimate uses — semantic constraints require filtering at the output level or prompt-level guidance, not logit bias.
. If the allowable output set is thousands of tokens, logit bias becomes impractical due to the 300-entry API limit. Consider fine-tuning or constrained decoding libraries instead.
When using providers that do not expose logit bias (
, some Gemini configurations). In these cases, grammar-guided generation, structured output APIs, or post-generation validation are the appropriate alternatives.

When fluency is critical and the constraint is broad. Banning too many common tokens forces the model into unnatural syntax and should be tested carefully against quality metrics before production deployment.
to convert human-readable token strings to integer IDs, then passes the resulting dictionary as
to the OpenAI API. Because words can tokenize differently depending on surrounding whitespace (

import tiktoken
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def build_logit_bias(
    model: str,
    ban: list[str] | None = None,
    boost: list[str] | None = None,
    boost_value: float = 5.0,
    ban_value: float = -100.0,
) -> dict[int, float]:
    """
    Build a logit_bias dict from human-readable token strings.

    Encodes each string with the model's tokenizer, handling both
    leading-space and no-space variants automatically.
    """
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    bias: dict[int, float] = {}

    def add_all_variants(word: str, value: float) -> None:
        # Encode with and without leading space to cover both contexts
        for variant in [word, " " + word]:
            for token_id in enc.encode(variant):
                # A single word can tokenize to multiple sub-tokens;
                # apply the bias to each sub-token individually.
                bias[token_id] = value

    for word in (ban or []):
        add_all_variants(word, ban_value)
    for word in (boost or []):
        add_all_variants(word, boost_value)

    return bias


# Example: force a yes/no response
yes_no_bias = build_logit_bias(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    boost=["yes", "no", "Yes", "No"],
    boost_value=20.0,
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer with only 'yes' or 'no'."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Is Python an interpreted language?"},
    ],
    max_tokens=1,
    logit_bias=yes_no_bias,
    temperature=0,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)  # "Yes" or "No"

constraint works together with logit bias here: it guarantees the model outputs exactly one token, and the bias ensures that token is one of the desired labels. This pattern is highly reliable for binary classification tasks that can be rephrased as yes/no questions.

import tiktoken
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini"


def build_allow_only_bias(model: str, allowed: list[str]) -> dict[int, float]:
    """
    Create a logit_bias that suppresses everything except the allowed tokens.
    Strategy: ban all ASCII single-character tokens, then whitelist the allowed ones.
    Works well when allowed tokens are single characters (A, B, C, D, etc.).
    """
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    bias: dict[int, float] = {}

    # Suppress A-Z, a-z, 0-9 as single-char tokens, and common answer prefixes
    for ch in "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789":
        ids = enc.encode(ch)
        if len(ids) == 1:
            bias[ids[0]] = -100.0

    # Whitelist only the desired choices
    for choice in allowed:
        for variant in [choice, " " + choice]:
            for tid in enc.encode(variant):
                bias[tid] = 20.0   # strong positive to dominate

    return bias


quiz_bias = build_allow_only_bias(MODEL, allowed=["A", "B", "C", "D"])

question = (
    "Which sorting algorithm has an average time complexity of O(n log n)?\n"
    "A) Bubble sort\nB) Merge sort\nC) Insertion sort\nD) Selection sort"
)

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model=MODEL,
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer with only the letter of the correct choice."},
        {"role": "user", "content": question},
    ],
    max_tokens=1,
    logit_bias=quiz_bias,
    temperature=0,
)

answer = resp.choices[0].message.content.strip()
print(f"Answer: {answer}")   # B
assert answer in ("A", "B", "C", "D"), f"Unexpected token: {answer!r}"

import tiktoken
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini"


def get_token_ids(model: str, words: list[str]) -> set[int]:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    ids: set[int] = set()
    for word in words:
        for variant in [word, " " + word, word.lower(), " " + word.lower()]:
            ids.update(enc.encode(variant))
    return ids


# Soft-penalise colloquial terms the clinical writing guide flags
informal_words = [
    "feeling", "tummy", "belly", "sick", "bug", "shot", "meds",
    "gets better", "fine", "okay", "ok",
]

bias = {tid: -5.0 for tid in get_token_ids(MODEL, informal_words)}

prompt = (
    "Summarize the following patient note in formal clinical language:\n"
    "Patient came in feeling sick to their tummy. Says the meds from last "
    "time didn't help and the bug is getting worse."
)

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model=MODEL,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    max_tokens=120,
    logit_bias=bias,
    temperature=0.3,
)

print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
# Expected: formal clinical phrasing, avoids 'tummy', 'sick', etc.

The soft bias of -5 reduces the probability of informal tokens without eliminating them entirely, allowing the model to use them when no clinical alternative is plausible. This is preferable to -100 for fluency preservation in open-ended generation.

A customer support automation platform routes incoming tickets to the correct department. The first-step classifier uses a single-token generation call with logit bias to categorize each ticket into one of five departments, then routes the ticket in real time.

import tiktoken
from openai import OpenAI
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = OpenAI()
MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini"

DEPARTMENTS = ["billing", "technical", "returns", "shipping", "general"]
DEPARTMENT_MAP = {d[0].upper(): d for d in DEPARTMENTS}   # B→billing, T→technical, ...

# Precompute bias at startup — reuse for every request
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(MODEL)

def _build_department_bias() -> dict[int, float]:
    bias: dict[int, float] = {}
    allowed_first_chars = {d[0].upper() for d in DEPARTMENTS}
    # Suppress all single-letter tokens except the initials of department names
    for ch in "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ":
        ids = enc.encode(ch)
        if ids:
            bias[ids[0]] = -100.0 if ch not in allowed_first_chars else 20.0
    # Suppress lowercase to avoid case ambiguity
    for ch in "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz":
        ids = enc.encode(ch)
        if ids:
            bias[ids[0]] = -100.0
    return bias

DEPT_BIAS = _build_department_bias()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are a ticket router. Given a customer message, output ONE LETTER "
    "representing the most appropriate department:\n"
    "B = billing  |  T = technical  |  R = returns  |  S = shipping  |  G = general"
)


@dataclass
class RoutingResult:
    letter: str
    department: str
    finish_reason: str


def route_ticket(message: str) -> RoutingResult:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": message},
        ],
        max_tokens=1,
        logit_bias=DEPT_BIAS,
        temperature=0,
    )
    letter = resp.choices[0].message.content.strip().upper()
    return RoutingResult(
        letter=letter,
        department=DEPARTMENT_MAP.get(letter, "unknown"),
        finish_reason=resp.choices[0].finish_reason,
    )


# --- Demo ---
tickets = [
    "I was charged twice on my last invoice.",
    "My device won't connect to Wi-Fi after the latest firmware update.",
    "I want to send back the headphones I bought last week.",
    "Where is my package? The tracking number shows it left the warehouse 3 days ago.",
    "I have a general question about your loyalty program.",
]

for ticket in tickets:
    result = route_ticket(ticket)
    print(f"[{result.letter}] {result.department:10s} | {ticket[:60]}")

In production this approach handles hundreds of tickets per second. The logit bias guarantees the model can only output one of five letters, eliminating the post-generation parsing step entirely. The bias is computed once at service startup and reused across all requests, incurring zero per-request overhead.
by encoding it without a leading space. In most contexts, words appear after a space, so the model tokenizes them as
(with a leading space) — a different token ID. Always encode both

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o-mini")

# WRONG: only bans one tokenization
wrong_bias = {enc.encode("violence")[0]: -100}

# CORRECT: bans all common variants
correct_ids = set()
for variant in ["violence", " violence", "Violence", " Violence"]:
    correct_ids.update(enc.encode(variant))
correct_bias = {tid: -100.0 for tid in correct_ids}

print("Wrong IDs:", list(wrong_bias.keys()))
print("Correct IDs:", sorted(correct_ids))
Using logit bias as the sole content safety mechanism

A bias of -100 on specific tokens cannot stop the model from expressing prohibited concepts through synonyms, paraphrases, or multi-token combinations that achieve the same meaning. Logit bias is a supplement to content moderation, not a replacement. Always pair it with output-level classifiers for safety-critical applications.
Applying extreme bias to common grammatical tokens

or aggressively biasing punctuation tokens forces the model into grammatically broken or incoherent output. Before deploying a ban list, inspect the frequency of each token in a representative sample of expected outputs to assess fluency impact.

Long words are often split into multiple subword tokens. Banning only the first subword token of a compound word does not prevent the model from generating the word — the subsequent subwords still have normal probabilities. For example,
, and every other word starting with 'un'. Use the full token ID list from encoding the complete word.

The OpenAI API silently truncates logit_bias dictionaries exceeding 300 entries or returns a validation error. Attempting to implement large vocabulary restrictions with logit bias will hit this limit. For vocabulary sets larger than ~100 tokens, consider alternatives such as constrained decoding libraries or grammar-guided generation.
Always use tiktoken for ID lookup.
Hard-coding token IDs is fragile — they can change between model versions even if the vocabulary string looks the same. Always encode at runtime using the target model's tokenizer.
For each word, encode at minimum: bare form, leading-space form, capitalized variants. Use a helper function so this is never omitted.
Prefer soft biases for fluency-sensitive tasks.
Reserve -100 for absolute bans. Use values in the range [-20, +20] for nudging the model toward or away from a vocabulary domain without destroying fluency.

Generate a sample of completions with the bias applied and inspect whether the desired tokens appear or disappear and whether fluency is preserved. A bias that looks correct may still allow a tokenization variant you missed.
The tiktoken encoding step and bias dictionary construction are deterministic. Compute them once during service initialization and cache them. Per-request construction is unnecessary overhead.
Combine with max_tokens=1 for single-token selection.
When the task requires choosing exactly one token from a small set, set max_tokens=1. The model cannot produce multi-token outputs that circumvent your bias list.

A bias that works well at temperature 0 may become less effective at higher temperatures, where the model's distribution is flatter and competing tokens have relatively higher baseline probability. If your deployment uses temperature > 0, test the bias at that temperature.
Document which tokens are biased and why.
Bias dictionaries can accumulate ad hoc additions over time. Maintain a comment or metadata entry for each biased token explaining the business rule it enforces.

The computational cost of logit bias is negligible relative to inference. The transformer forward pass is the bottleneck — it runs on GPU and accounts for nearly all latency. Logit bias is applied after the forward pass and involves at most 300 additions on a vector, a CPU-side operation taking microseconds.
is part of the request JSON, not the response. A dictionary with 300 entries adds roughly 3 KB to the request payload, well within normal HTTP bounds.

For high-throughput deployments, precomputing the bias dictionary at startup is the only optimization needed. There is no per-request overhead beyond the JSON serialization of the dictionary (negligible).

When using logit bias with streaming (stream=True), the bias applies identically at every generation step. There is no additional latency introduced by streaming — the bias is applied server-side before tokens are sent to the client.
For self-hosted models using vLLM or llama.cpp, the logit bias injection point is the same: additive offsets applied to the logit vector before sampling. vLLM exposes

— a list of callable hooks that receive the logit tensor and can modify it in place. This is more powerful than the dictionary API (no 300-entry limit, full tensor access) but requires more implementation work.

# vLLM example: using logits_processor for logit bias
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
from vllm.model_executor.guided_decoding.logits_processor import LogitsProcessor
import torch


class SimpleLogitBias(LogitsProcessor):
    """Custom logits processor that mimics the OpenAI logit_bias dict."""

    def __init__(self, bias: dict[int, float]):
        self.bias_tensor = None
        self._bias_dict = bias

    def _ensure_tensor(self, logits: torch.Tensor) -> None:
        if self.bias_tensor is None:
            self.bias_tensor = torch.zeros(logits.shape[-1], dtype=logits.dtype,
                                           device=logits.device)
            for token_id, value in self._bias_dict.items():
                self.bias_tensor[token_id] = value

    def __call__(self, input_ids: list[int], scores: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        self._ensure_tensor(scores)
        return scores + self.bias_tensor


# Usage
llm = LLM(model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")
sampling_params = SamplingParams(
    temperature=0,
    max_tokens=1,
    logits_processors=[SimpleLogitBias({3869: 20.0, 2822: 20.0})],  # "yes"/"no" IDs
)
outputs = llm.generate(["Is the sky blue?"], sampling_params)
print(outputs[0].outputs[0].text)
logit_bias: dict[int, float], max 300 entries, range [-100, 100]
Production-ready, works with gpt-4o and gpt-4o-mini
The required companion library for looking up token IDs by model
Full tensor access; no 300-entry limit; higher implementation effort
Works with GGUF models; same semantics as OpenAI API
Full flexibility; apply token-level additive bias or arbitrary transformations
Higher-level abstraction; enforces grammars rather than raw token biases
Grammar and regex-guided generation using logit processors under the hood
For Hugging Face Transformers, logit bias is implemented via

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, LogitsProcessor
from transformers import LogitsProcessorList
import torch


class DictLogitBias(LogitsProcessor):
    """Additive logit bias applied at every generation step."""

    def __init__(self, bias: dict[int, float], device: str = "cpu"):
        self.bias = torch.zeros(1, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)  # placeholder
        self._raw = bias
        self._device = device
        self._initialized = False

    def _init(self, vocab_size: int) -> None:
        self.bias = torch.zeros(vocab_size, dtype=torch.float32, device=self._device)
        for tok_id, val in self._raw.items():
            self.bias[tok_id] = val
        self._initialized = True

    def __call__(self, input_ids: torch.LongTensor, scores: torch.FloatTensor) -> torch.FloatTensor:
        if not self._initialized:
            self._init(scores.shape[-1])
        return scores + self.bias.to(scores.device)


# Load a small model for demonstration
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

# Bias "good" strongly positive, "bad" strongly negative
bias = {
    tokenizer.encode("good")[0]: 15.0,
    tokenizer.encode("bad")[0]: -15.0,
}

processor_list = LogitsProcessorList([DictLogitBias(bias)])

inputs = tokenizer("The result was", return_tensors="pt")
output = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=5,
    logits_processor=processor_list,
    do_sample=False,
)
print(tokenizer.decode(output[0]))
Logit bias is one of several techniques for constraining or guiding LLM output at generation time. Understanding when each is appropriate prevents misuse.
Additive offset to raw logits per token ID
Low if used sparingly; high if overused
Masking logits based on a grammar/regex state machine
Train model weights to avoid or prefer patterns
Validate or reject generated text after completion
Grammar-guided logit masking for valid JSON
Prune beam paths that violate constraints

Logit bias is the right choice when: the constraint maps cleanly to a small number of specific tokens, the provider supports it, and the constraint must be enforced at generation time rather than post-hoc. Grammar-guided generation is superior for complex structural constraints (nested JSON, SQL). Fine-tuning is superior for pervasive stylistic or safety constraints that must generalize to novel inputs. Output filtering is simplest for semantic constraints when latency allows regeneration.
Logit bias does not prevent prompt injection into semantic content.

A user who tricks the model into believing it is in a different mode can still cause it to generate semantically problematic outputs through synonyms and paraphrases, even if specific tokens are banned. Logit bias is not a security boundary — it is a generation hint.
Token ID drift between model versions.
When OpenAI releases a new version of a model, the underlying tokenizer may change, causing token IDs to shift. A bias dictionary built against
may not map to the same tokens in a later version. Always re-validate bias dictionaries when upgrading model versions.

If a bias dictionary inadvertently bans tokens that are required for the model to continue coherently (e.g., common punctuation or high-frequency grammatical words), the model may enter a degenerate generation loop, repetitively producing the least-penalized tokens available. Monitor generation quality metrics and implement maximum token limits to prevent runaway outputs.

Some API implementations silently ignore logit_bias entries beyond the maximum count rather than raising an error. If a bias dictionary is built dynamically (e.g., from a user-configurable list), always verify the final size before sending the request.

In an agentic pipeline where one step's output becomes the next step's input, a logit bias that forces a specific token in step N may not be appropriate for all states that reach step N. Ensure that bias dictionaries are step-specific, not applied globally across all generation calls in the pipeline.

Before relying on logit bias for safety-critical constraints, test with prompts designed to elicit the banned content through indirect routes. Logit bias should be one layer in a defense-in-depth strategy, combined with system-prompt hardening and output-level classification.

Imagine the model's vocabulary as a scoreboard with one lane per token — roughly 100,000 lanes for a model like GPT-4o. After the forward pass, each lane displays a raw score (logit). The softmax step converts those raw scores into vote shares that sum to 100 %. The sampler then picks the lane that wins the weighted lottery.

Logit bias is like a handicap system applied before the softmax converts scores to vote shares. You can add points to any lane (making it more likely to win) or subtract points (making it less likely). Subtracting 100 points is so extreme that no realistic raw score can overcome it — that lane is effectively disqualified. Adding 20 points to one lane might make it 1,000 times more likely to win than a competing lane with a similar raw score.
The critical insight is that the adjustment happens

the vote-share calculation. Once softmax runs, the distribution is normalized — adding bias after softmax would require re-normalizing, which would not be equivalent. The additive-before-softmax formulation means that bias values interact with each other: adding +20 to five tokens simultaneously makes them all more likely
, but does not change their relative odds with respect to each other.

This is why extreme positive biases can degrade generation quality even for tasks where you only care about the top token: if five tokens all have a bias of +100, their relative probabilities are determined entirely by their raw logits, and the model may be unable to choose between them deterministically, leading to inconsistent outputs at temperature > 0.
Logit bias adds a scalar to selected tokens' raw logits before softmax:
. A value of -100 effectively bans a token; +100 near-forces it.
. Always use the model's tokenizer (tiktoken for OpenAI models) to look them up, and always encode multiple variants (with and without leading space, different capitalizations).
relative to inference — the bias addition is a trivial sparse vector operation compared to the GPU-bound transformer forward pass.

constraints, not semantic ones. It cannot prevent the model from expressing a concept through synonyms, paraphrases, or multi-token alternatives. Use it for lexical constraints, not content safety in isolation.
The OpenAI API limits logit_bias dictionaries to
and values to [-100, 100]. For larger vocabulary restrictions, use grammar-guided generation or constrained decoding libraries.
for fluency-sensitive tasks. Reserve -100 for absolute bans. Monitor output fluency when applying bans to ensure no critical grammatical tokens are inadvertently suppressed.
when combined with max_tokens=1 for single-token selection tasks (binary classification, category routing). This pattern eliminates multi-token workarounds entirely.
For self-hosted models, logit bias is implemented through
— callable hooks applied to the logit tensor before sampling. This provides full flexibility with no dictionary size limit.
after model version upgrades. Token ID assignments can change between versions, silently breaking constraints that appeared to work on the previous version.
